section.title: Isaac and Abimelech
section.sectionRationale: This is the only chapter given wholly to Isaac, and it shows the promise made to Abraham renewed to the son. Isaac repeats his father's faith and his father's failures, God blesses him amid famine and strife over wells, and a pagan king again seeks a treaty because God is plainly with him.
section.thesis: God renews the covenant promise to Isaac and blesses him abundantly, so that even his enemies confess the LORD is with him; the God of Abraham proves faithful to the next generation, carrying His promise on despite famine, fear, and conflict.
section.frameNote: Six scenes: the promise confirmed to Isaac; the sister-wife deception; prosperity and contested wells; the LORD's appearing at Beersheba; the covenant with Abimelech; Esau's grievous marriages.

units.label: The promise confirmed to Isaac
units.note: I will be with you and bless you

verses.text: Now there was another famine in the land, subsequent to the one that had occurred in Abraham’s time. And Isaac went to Abimelech king of the Philistines at Gerar.
The LORD appeared to Isaac and said, “Do not go down to Egypt. Settle in the land where I tell you.

Stay in this land as a foreigner, and I will be with you and {{barak|bless}} you. For I will give all these lands to you and your offspring, and I will confirm the oath that I swore to your father Abraham.
I will make your descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky, and I will give them all these lands, and through your offspring all nations of the earth will be blessed,
because Abraham listened to My voice and kept My charge, My commandments, My statutes, and My laws.”
So Isaac settled in Gerar.

But when the men of that place asked about his wife, he said, “She is my sister.” For he was afraid to say, “She is my wife,” since he thought to himself, “The men of this place will kill me on account of Rebekah, because she is so beautiful.”
When Isaac had been there a long time, Abimelech king of the Philistines looked down from the window and was surprised to see Isaac caressing his wife Rebekah.
Abimelech sent for Isaac and said, “So she is really your wife! How could you say, ‘She is my sister’?” Isaac replied, “Because I thought I might die on account of her.”
“What is this you have done to us?” asked Abimelech. “One of the people could easily have slept with your wife, and you would have brought guilt upon us.”
So Abimelech warned all the people, saying, “Whoever harms this man or his wife will surely be put to death.”
Now Isaac sowed seed in the land, and that very year he reaped a hundredfold. And the LORD blessed him,
and he became richer and richer, until he was exceedingly wealthy.
He owned so many flocks and herds and servants that the Philistines envied him.
So the Philistines took dirt and stopped up all the wells that his father’s servants had dug in the days of his father Abraham.
Then Abimelech said to Isaac, “Depart from us, for you are much too powerful for us.”
So Isaac left that place and encamped in the Valley of Gerar and settled there.

Isaac reopened the wells that had been dug in the days of his father Abraham, which the Philistines had stopped up after Abraham died. And he gave these wells the same names his father had given them.
Then Isaac’s servants dug in the valley and found a well of fresh water there.
But the herdsmen of Gerar quarreled with Isaac’s herdsmen and said, “The water is ours!” So he named the well Esek, because they contended with him.
Then they dug another well and quarreled over that one also; so he named it Sitnah.
He moved on from there and dug another well, and they did not quarrel over it. He named it {{rachab|Rehoboth}} and said, “At last the LORD has made room for us, and we will be fruitful in the land.”

units.label: The LORD appears at Beersheba
units.note: do not be afraid, for I am with you

verses.text: From there Isaac went up to Beersheba,

and that night the LORD appeared to him and said, “I am the God of your father Abraham. Do not be afraid, for I am with you. I will bless you and multiply your descendants for the sake of My servant Abraham.”
So Isaac built an altar there and called on the name of the LORD, and he pitched his tent there. His servants also dug a well there.

units.label: The covenant with Abimelech
units.note: the LORD has been with you

verses.text: Later, Abimelech came to Isaac from Gerar, with Ahuzzath his adviser and Phicol the commander of his army.
“Why have you come to me?” Isaac asked them. “You hated me and sent me away.”
“We can plainly see that the LORD has been with you,” they replied. “We recommend that there should now be an oath between us and you. Let us make a {{berith|covenant}} with you
that you will not harm us, just as we have not harmed you but have done only good to you, sending you on your way in peace. And now you are blessed by the LORD.”
So Isaac prepared a feast for them, and they ate and drank.
And they got up early the next morning and swore an oath to each other. Then Isaac sent them on their way, and they left him in peace.
On that same day, Isaac’s servants came and told him about the well they had dug. “We have found water!” they told him.
So he called it Shibah, and to this day the name of the city is Beersheba.

units.label: Esau's grievous marriages
units.note: a grief to Isaac and Rebekah

verses.text: When Esau was forty years old, he took as his wives Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite and Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite.
And they brought grief to Isaac and Rebekah.

barak.gloss: To bless, to bestow life giving favour and good, from a root linked to kneeling. God's blessing is His active goodness that makes fruitful and prospers. The word runs like a refrain through this chapter, as God blesses Isaac in famine, in harvest, and in the eyes of watching enemies.

rachab.gloss: To be wide, broad, or roomy, to make space. Isaac names a well Rehoboth, broad places, because at last the LORD has made room for him after repeated conflict. The word pictures God enlarging His people's place and giving room to be fruitful when the way has been cramped by strife.

berith.gloss: A binding agreement, treaty, or covenant, often sealed with an oath, a meal, or a sacrifice. Here it is a peace pact Abimelech seeks with Isaac because God is with him. The same word carries the far weightier covenant God Himself makes and confirms with the patriarchs, and finally the new covenant in Christ.

basic.context: This chapter follows Isaac through a famine, a lie about his wife, disputes over wells, and a treaty with Abimelech. Woven through it all, God renews to Isaac the very promises He made to Abraham. The key question is whether God's covenant will hold with the son, and the chapter answers with a resounding yes despite Isaac's flaws.
basic.hermeneutics: This is history that also teaches. Isaac clearly echoes Abraham, both his faith and his failures, and the writer wants us to see the promise passing intact to the next generation. The question is how God's faithfulness relates to the patriarchs' weakness, and the answer centres on grace, not merit.
basic.originalLanguages: The word barak, to bless, sounds again and again as God prospers Isaac. When strife over water finally eases, Isaac names a well Rehoboth, from rachab, to make room, praising God for room to be fruitful. Abimelech then seeks a berith, a covenant of peace, confessing that the LORD is with Isaac.
basic.typology: Isaac, the son who inherits his father's promise, foreshadows the greater Son who inherits all the promises of God. The blessing that flows to the nations through Isaac's offspring points to Christ, and the room God makes at Rehoboth pictures the wide welcome of God's grace.
basic.christ: The promise renewed to Isaac, that through his offspring all nations will be blessed, reaches its goal in Christ. Where Isaac lied out of fear, Christ spoke the truth even at the cost of His life, and He is the true Seed in whom the covenant is finally secured.
basic.god: God is faithful, present, and generous. He renews His oath to Isaac for Abraham's sake, blesses him in famine, and makes room for him amid conflict. He is the God who says do not be afraid, for I am with you, and He makes even enemies acknowledge His presence.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 12 and 22 hold the promises God renews here. Genesis 20 and 21 record Abraham's parallel sister-wife episode and treaty. Romans 4 celebrates the promise to Abraham's offspring, fulfilled in Christ.
basic.oneStory: God carries His promise safely from Abraham to Isaac, blessing him so richly that even his enemies see the LORD is with him. The covenant advances by grace despite human weakness, moving on toward Christ, in whom all nations are blessed.

context.literary: The one chapter devoted entirely to Isaac, built on deliberate echoes of the Abraham stories: famine, a sister-wife ruse, disputed wells, and a covenant with Abimelech at Beersheba. The repetitions are the point, showing the promise handed on intact to the son, framed by two divine appearances that renew the covenant.
context.historical: Wells were lifelines in the arid Negev, and control of them meant survival, which explains the fierce disputes. Treaties sealed by oath and shared meals, and the naming of wells and places after events, all reflect the customs of the patriarchal world.
context.canonical: The chapter restates the Abrahamic promises of land, offspring, and blessing to the nations, Genesis 12:1-3, now to Isaac. It stands within the covenant thread that runs to David and to Christ, in whom the promise that all nations will be blessed is fulfilled, Galatians 3:8.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the covenant promise passing faithfully from Abraham to Isaac, to portray Isaac as both an heir of faith and a man who repeats his father's fear, and to display God's unwavering blessing that even enemies must acknowledge.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Isaac's actions, including his lie about Rebekah; it does not commend the deception. The abiding truths are God's faithfulness to His promise, His presence with His people, and the peace and room He provides.

debates.question: Why does the narrator record Isaac repeating Abraham's sister-wife deception rather than quietly omitting it?

views.label: To show the patriarchs' real weakness
views.case: Scripture tells the truth about its heroes; Isaac's fear driven lie exposes that the promise rests on God's grace, not on flawless faith.

views.label: To magnify God's protecting faithfulness
views.case: Precisely by recording the failure and God's rescue, the narrative highlights that God guards His covenant and His people even when they falter.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree: the honesty about Isaac's sin underscores that the covenant advances by God's mercy and faithfulness, which is exactly why enemies end up confessing the LORD is with him.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's heartbeat is barak, to bless: God blesses Isaac in famine, in a hundredfold harvest, and before watching rivals. After the bitter wells named Esek, contention, and Sitnah, hostility, Isaac finally names one Rehoboth, from rachab, to make room, rejoicing that the LORD has given him space to be fruitful. The chapter climaxes as Abimelech seeks a berith, a covenant of peace, confessing what the reader already knows, that the LORD is with Isaac, so that even the language of the enemy testifies to God's blessing.

typology.type: Isaac the son inheriting his father's promise
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Son and heir of all the promises of God, in whom every promise is Yes, 2 Corinthians 1:20.

typology.type: Rehoboth, the room God makes after strife
typology.fulfillment: The spacious welcome of God's grace in Christ, who gives His people room and makes them fruitful, John 15:5.

study.christ: The promise God renews to Isaac, that through his offspring all the nations of the earth will be blessed, finds its fulfilment in Christ, the true offspring of Abraham. Isaac lied out of fear to save his life; Jesus, the greater Son, spoke the truth before His accusers and laid down His life to secure the covenant. The blessing that made even enemies confess the LORD was with Isaac points to the greater blessing in which the far off nations are drawn to God through His Son. And the room God made at Rehoboth foreshadows the wide grace in which Christ makes His people fruitful.
study.god: God is faithful, present, and abundantly generous. He renews His oath to Isaac for the sake of His servant Abraham, showing that the covenant rests on His own commitment. He blesses Isaac through famine and strife, tells him not to fear because He is with him, and makes room for him when the way is cramped. His blessing is so evident that his enemies seek peace, acknowledging that the LORD is with him.

crossReferences.note: The original promise of land, offspring, and blessing to the nations, here renewed to Isaac.
Abraham's parallel sister-wife episode with Abimelech, which Isaac's deception clearly echoes.
The oath God swore to Abraham that He now confirms to Isaac.
The gospel promise that in Abraham all nations would be blessed, fulfilled in Christ, the promised offspring.

study.oneStory: God carries His covenant safely from Abraham to Isaac, blessing him so richly amid famine and conflict that even his enemies confess the LORD is with him. The promise advances by grace despite Isaac's fear and failure, moving steadily toward Christ, the true offspring of Abraham, in whom all the nations of the earth are blessed.

quiz.q: What did the LORD promise Isaac during the famine?

options.text: To be with him, bless him, and confirm the oath He had sworn to Abraham.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 renew the Abrahamic promises directly to Isaac.

options.text: That the famine would end within a single year.
options.note: God promises His presence and blessing; He does not simply date the famine's end.

options.text: That Isaac should go down to Egypt for safety.
options.note: God tells him the opposite, not to go down to Egypt but to stay in the land.

options.text: That the land would pass to the Philistines.
options.note: God promises the land to Isaac and his offspring, not to the Philistines.

quiz.q: How did Isaac repeat one of his father Abraham's failures?

options.text: He lied that Rebekah was his sister out of fear for his life.
options.note: Verse 7 echoes Abraham's sister-wife deception, driven by fear.

options.text: He offered his son as a sacrifice.
options.note: That was Abraham's test in chapter 22; Isaac's echoed failure here is the sister-wife lie.

options.text: He drove Hagar and Ishmael away.
options.note: That belonged to Abraham's household, not Isaac's; his repeated failure is the lie about his wife.

options.text: He refused to leave Egypt.
options.note: Isaac never goes to Egypt; God tells him to stay in the land.

quiz.q: Why did Isaac name a well Rehoboth?

options.text: Because the LORD had at last made room for him after repeated quarrels over water.
options.note: Rehoboth means broad places; Isaac praises God for room to be fruitful.

options.text: Because it was the first well his servants ever dug.
options.note: It was not the first; it followed the disputed wells Esek and Sitnah.

options.text: Because the Philistines had given it to him as a gift.
options.note: The Philistines contended over the earlier wells; Rehoboth was one they did not quarrel over, not a gift.

options.text: Because it marked the place of a battle he had won.
options.note: It marks peace and room, not a battle.

quiz.q: Why did Abimelech come to make a covenant with Isaac?

options.text: Because he could plainly see that the LORD was with Isaac.
options.note: Verse 28 gives their stated reason, echoing the earlier treaty with Abraham.

options.text: Because Isaac had defeated him in war.
options.note: There is no war; Abimelech seeks peace because God is evidently with Isaac.

options.text: Because Isaac demanded tribute from Gerar.
options.note: Isaac makes no such demand; Abimelech comes seeking a peace oath.

options.text: Because he wanted to take Rebekah as his wife.
options.note: Abimelech had earlier protected Rebekah; the covenant is about peace, not marriage.

quiz.q: What does this chapter show about God's covenant promise?

options.text: It passes faithfully to Isaac by God's grace, holding firm despite Isaac's fear and failures.
options.note: The promise rests on God's faithfulness, not on Isaac's flawless obedience.

options.text: It depended on Isaac never sinning.
options.note: Isaac does sin, yet the promise stands; it rests on grace, not sinlessness.

options.text: It was transferred away from Isaac to Abimelech.
options.note: The promise remains with Isaac; Abimelech only seeks peace with him.

options.text: It ended because of the famine and the strife.
options.note: The covenant is renewed and confirmed through the famine and strife, not ended.

questions: God told Isaac not to fear because He was with him. Where do you most need to hear that same word of God's presence in your life right now?
Isaac fell into the very fear driven lie his father had told. What patterns from those before you do you find yourself repeating, and how might God break the cycle?
After strife over wells, Isaac praised God for making room at Rehoboth. Where are you longing for God to make room and give peace after a season of conflict?
Isaac's blessing was so evident that even his enemies sought peace with him. What would it look like for others to see clearly that God is with you?
God kept His promise to Isaac despite Isaac's weakness. How does it free you to know that God's faithfulness to you does not rest on your perfect performance?

translationNotes.note: The wells are named for what happened at them: Esek means contention, Sitnah means hostility, and Rehoboth means broad places or room, tracing Isaac's path from strife to peace.
Shibah plays on the Hebrew words for oath and for seven, tying the well's name to the sworn covenant and to the earlier naming of Beersheba in Abraham's day.
